[06:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Show Rate Benchmarks & Best Practices"
NÚMEROS: 50% show rate, 35% show rate, $50,000 offer, 65-80% show rate (2024 benchmark)
VISUAL: Comparação de diferentes taxas de show rate com contextos de preço, linha do tempo 2024, indicadores de performance aceitável vs. ideal
[06:20] Okay. Now, to be clear, every client that we have that has a 65 to 80% show rate in 2024, literally right now has dialed in what I'm about to talk about. Okay? So, number one, you have to get a lot more aggressive with the quantity of emails that you're sending to people. Um, in addition to that, the quantity of text reminders that you're going to send to people.
[06:45] So, the worst thing that you could do is just use a reminder-based sequence and increase the frequency of it. That is not what I'm talking about here. Uh to be clear on that, reminder-based uh emails and texts are, hey, you got to call at this time, you know, make sure you show up. That is not what I'm talking about, okay?
[07:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Reminder-Based vs. Selling-Based Sequences"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Comparação visual entre sequências de lembrete (X vermelho) vs. sequências de venda (check verde), ícones de email e SMS, diferenciação clara entre abordagens
[07:15] You really have to master being able to sell each step of the process. So, look at it like this. And this is this is the deficiency and thinking that most people have that suffer with low show rates. They think because somebody booked the call that they are sold on showing up for that call. That's not true.
[07:35] Okay, here's what is true. You were able to sell the person on booking a call. Now, you need to sell the person on showing up for the call. Okay? When you master that specific way of thinking about your show rate sequences and what you do to attack people to get them to show up, everything changes. The show rate improves dramatically.
[07:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Two-Step Sales Process"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Dois estágios separados: 1) Vender o agendamento da call, 2) Vender o comparecimento na call, setas conectando os estágios, ícones de calendário e pessoa presente
[08:10] And the sales team has to be with this same exact thinking. It starts with the owner of course transitions into the sales manager and has to carry over to the salespeople and the setters. Okay, you cannot neglect all the best practices that have to be implemented across the board here. But it starts with that mental frame. Okay, of everybody has to be on the same page. This person is not sold yet on showing up. Okay.
[08:35] Now, to be clear right away when you start thinking that way, one of the first things that becomes very obvious is that the standard reminder-based email and text sequences are essentially worthless. Okay? They don't really do a lot. Somebody doesn't need reminded shit 20 times to increase the probability of them showing up for when they booked. Okay?
[08:55] If anything, that's just going to be annoying. You know,
[08:58] that's exactly right. It increases the probability of cancellations, of no shows. It actually increases the probability of no shows. It doesn't decrease it. And that comes from testing to be clear, not some made-up shit

NÚMEROS: $100,000 agency deal (exemplo mencionado)
VISUAL: Diagrama mostrando gap percebido entre guru e prospect, setas de distância, ícones de pessoa em diferentes níveis de sucesso
[17:40] They don't see all the steps that literally I myself went through to get to where I'm at and how they could take those same steps, too. So, what do I got to do? Well, I got to show somebody else who's closer to where they're at that's started to take the steps and is experiencing the initial results of what I claim I can help them with. Okay.
[18:00] So, in Jesse's case, once again, consistent, profitable, funded trader. When somebody is none of those things, and Jesse's way over here getting who knows how much money from those things, we have to show people who are closer to where that person's starting at.

[20:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Testimonial Selection Strategy"
NÚMEROS: $100,000 payouts (muito avançado para iniciantes)
VISUAL: Escada de progressão com diferentes níveis de testimonials, X vermelho nos testimonials muito avançados, check verde nos testimonials de iniciantes, setas mostrando proximidade ideal
[20:55] This is this is this happens all the time. If you go to almost any fucking website for any program on earth, they pick people who have already achieved a like the most substantial result out of everybody who's ever bought from them. They don't ever highlight the people who are only a few steps away from where the person buying is at.
[21:15] Those testimonials have a substantially greater impact than the ones that are closer to where you are. Does that make sense? So to be clear, you cannot pick you cannot cherry pick the big dogs. You cherry pick the people that are just getting started. And ideally, they're overcoming objections in their testimonial that the person who's considering buying has right now. Okay.
[21:38] So let's give some other examples. So in trading, one of the most common things is either number one emotional trading, number two revenge trading. Okay. Let me give you examples of those. If you're sitting there trading and you lose money, all right, sometimes the loss is bigger than what you wanted it to be.
[21:58] Or sometimes even if you just lose in general, you carry over some general good business principles like, well, I'm not going to walk away a loser, you know, I'm going to put in the work necessary to get back to my positive position. That is terrible mindset when you're trading. That can fuck you tremendously. Okay.
[22:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Common Trading Objections"
NÚMEROS: 2 principais: #1 Emotional Trading, #2 Revenge Trading
VISUAL: Dois cenários problemáticos ilustrados, ícones de emoções negativas, ciclo vicioso de perdas, contas explodidas (blown accounts)
[22:25] Now, to be clear, revenge trading in its simplest form is, okay, you lost money, you're going to try to trade to get it back, and you end up getting deeper and deeper in the hole. And then when you trade with these prop firms, or technically even with your own money, you have the ability to blow your account, which means you lost enough to have to start the whole process over again.
[22:48] So, that's a very common thing that Jesse's buyers are going to deal with. So, same exact logic. If I can sit there and cherrypick, okay, a testimonial that's only a few steps ahead of where the buyer is that says, you know, before I bought Jesse's program, my most common reason I'd blow accounts was I just I had no control over myself.
[23:10] I would be emotionally erratic. Like, I was all over the place. When I was down, I would revenge trade and I would just bury myself into a deeper hole and blow so many accounts. when I would be up, I would keep trading and then I'd actually lose what I made because I just I thought it could get better and better and it and it just wouldn't and that would that would screw me over.
[23:35] And you know, I I kept blaming other people. I kept blaming the prop firms. I kept I you know, I just blamed everything but myself. I blamed my connection with the fucking brokerage accounts. Like any anything else I could point the finger at, I would.
[23:50] And you know, then then I saw a few Jesse videos and you know, I took the risk at that point. I I had nothing to lose. you know, I decided to pull the trigger on it and I'm pleased to say I've been in the program for about six weeks now and I've gotten almost full control over myself. I never revenge trade anymore. I'm no longer emotional. I I'm patient. I wait for the right setups.

[28:20] because this is usually stuff that you are posting organically and it becomes an evergreen part of your sales process when you use it the way that I'll demonstrate to you here. [29:08] That means that out of 24 videos that I'll show you here shortly, 7,100 people have seen nine different videos within about 72 hours. Okay. Now, to be clear, just right off the bat, do you think that that positively or negatively impacts my probability of getting somebody to show up?
[29:28] So, let me put this in perspective. This is a dynamically updating audience in a CRM. In this particular case, HubSpot, we have an audience that when somebody books a call, they get added into and then about 14 days after they booked that call, they get removed from the audience.

[29:50] So, this is a 14-day rolling audience that people get added to and removed from. I widened it to 14 days so I had enough people to be able to target. [30:15] Some of you guys are operating where you have like fucking 10 or 12. You need at least a hundred just to just to get the audience. [33:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Framing vs. Objection Handling"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[33:28] Okay, I'm gonna say it again. Framing is teaching people how to think that benefit your buying cycle. Okay, let me give you some examples of that. If I have a piece of content that encourages action taking, okay, one thing I see all the time in people who suck in life is high levels of skepticism.
[33:48] So, I always say skepticism ruins your life and your potential. Okay? It's one of the number one things that holds people back from making a lot of money. Being skeptical. Okay? Now, being skeptical had its evolutionary place in our lives. If we were going to eat a berry off of a bush, that shit could be poisonous and kill us. It's good to be skeptical about a berry on a bush that we've never eaten before, right?
[34:12] But when I have an income opportunity that could potentially change my life and generations of my family's lives to follow, I wouldn't want to allow skepticism to potentially hold me back there, would I? So, as an example, I could literally make a content piece just like that where I'm like, look, I could start the video by saying, hey, skepticism ruins your life. It will ruin your potential.

[50:00] I when I looked up I'm like, "Okay, is this possible? Is this realistic? Am I going to get fucking scammed by doing this shit?" Like, what's the reality of this situation? [52:55] But, um, anyway, long story short, all 20 comments were like, "No, don't show up to the call. That's a scam. No, don't buy the product. That's a scam." Uh, you can literally find all that for free on YouTube. Like, no, that doesn't make any sense. Like, don't do it. That was all 20 comments. There wasn't a single thing that was positive propaganda in any of that.
[53:18] Um, to be clear, what's a great thing for that company to do? [53:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Reddit Reputation Management"
NÚMEROS: 20 negative comments → 0 positive responses
VISUAL: Estratégia de mobilização de comunidade, estudantes respondendo a haters, balanceamento de narrativa, transformação de propaganda negativa
[53:50] None of those people bought that person's program. None of those people are clients. They're just the classic cliche like, you know, oh no, don't do it. to fucking scam crowd. Okay, not even real people. But it's like that's Do you think that's a part of the research phase that a potential person showing up for a call that's going to cost $24,000 is going to go out of their way and look up?
[54:12] If it's not before the call, it's after the call when they're considering buying. For sure. You see what I mean? You have to be your own company's positive propaganda machine when you fail to do that.
[54:25] So, let me give you another example. I think Jeremy too because you mentioned at the mastermind uh the book propaganda as well.
[54:35] Yeah. So the people weren't there just in case Jeremy recommended it. So
[54:40] great
[54:42] talks a lot about this as well.
[54:45] Now to be clear it's it's important to note okay how many times have you bought something in your life where you've seen maybe one or two negative things but you've had an overwhelming amount of positive things that you've seen in your consideration phase and you bought it anyway.
[55:02] Oh yeah. I' I'd say almost everything to be quite fair in some way.
[55:08] It's like, you know, there's people how Okay, I'll give you an example. How many of you have heard terrible things about McLarens? [55:28] Yeah.
[55:30] Why do those people still buy McLarens?
[55:32] Because the lure it gives. They're fast.
[55:38] No, no. Break it down on a more technical level. They likely have seen more positive things than negative things about McLaren.

NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Balança com propaganda positiva superando negativa, exemplo de McLaren (negativo conhecido mas ainda vende), princípio de volume de impressões
[55:52] Right. Yeah.
[55:55] So, look at it like a scale. Okay. If neutral falls into a particular category, meaning they don't see anything, I technically want you to count that as the bad part of the scale. Okay? You have to be your own company's positive propaganda. That's why the email frequency is so high.
[56:12] That's why our frequency on Facebook and Instagram with our content remarketing has got to be high because we want to have a far greater impression count of positive propaganda impressions comparatively to the handful of things they might see of skeptics, non buyers talking shit you know, or potentially even bad things that your company's done in the past.

You know, you might be one of those companies. You know what I mean? So anyway, my point being positive propaganda machine. Okay, that's where all this comes in. Does that make sense?
[57:02] 100%. Yeah. Now, this is gold. Absolute fucking gold, man.
[57:08] Okay. Do you guys have any questions? I uh I've got to go look at that penthouse here shortly, and I've got to dish out a 100 G's to fucking move. So, uh, yeah, that's all.
[57:20] Just quickly, Jeremy, do you ever alter the amount you slam them on emails based on market sophistication, like if they're a higher level buyer,
[57:30] I will not do that for, uh, investment companies that we raise capital for. I will do that at a much lower frequency and I will increase the sophistication of the communications. Um, yes, with a a lower financially qualified demographic, I will I will typically be more aggressive on the frequency and I won't be as uh proper versus uh you know, once again as a direct example specifically with the raising capital crowd.
[58:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Market Sophistication Adjustments"
NÚMEROS: $100K+ (investment crowd example)
VISUAL: Dois níveis de abordagem: 1) High sophistication (lower frequency, PPMs, decks, compliance), 2) Lower sophistication (higher frequency, mais agressivo)
[58:05] Um, like I said, I mean, when I when I use the word sophistication, it simply means like the vocabulary is different. Uh, we might we might send them over like fucking PPMs or decks. Uh, we might not have as like, you know, I I might not even have video testimonials to use. I might not have like any kind of testimonial I can use.
[58:25] I might have some specific way I could demonstrate like historical annualized returns. Um, I might I might have like some kind of visuals for like numbers and shit like that. There's a lot of SEC compliance I have to follow as well in the investment care. So, yeah, to your point, I can't uh I can't communicate the same way for a more sophisticated demographic.
[58:45] It's important to note though, um even in a demographic where I'm teaching people to buy businesses, that's still at a 10 to $15,000 price point, something that I would consider as like lower sophistication because I'm not teaching existing business owners how to go buy more businesses. I'm teaching people how to buy one of their first businesses.
[59:08] And my point being um that that crowd is like the busier a person is in my opinion the frequency needs to elevate. The less busy a person is my frequency minimum is the 10 to 15.
[59:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Busy vs. Not Busy Frequency"
NÚMEROS: 10-15 emails (mínimo base)
VISUAL: Correlação inversa: Mais ocupado = Maior frequência necessária, Menos ocupado = Frequência mínima (10-15), ajuste baseado em estilo de vida do prospect
[59:30] Two quick questions just either and you can say hey save it I got to go to the penthouse. What does the first email consist of and do you do the same exact sequence through SMS?
[59:42] No, I do not do the same exact sequence through SMS. SMS I try to stick to uh one sentence with links to videos. Um and then reminders via text and uh email. I mean, look, the first email is typically going to be something along the lines of like, "Hey, you booked your call. You know, here's all the data. Here's a button to add it to your calendar just in case it didn't populate in there."
[1:00:08] And then I immediately go into all the framing content I just articulate.
[1:00:12] Right. Yeah. I meant that I guess I should have said second cuz like that's like I look at Yeah. So
[1:00:18] So dude, you you'd be surprised like the second email and beyond like they don't even look like you booked a call. They just look like I'm fucking hammering you with ways I want you to think. [laughter]

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
Show rate atual do Jesse: 50% (self-booking) vs. 96% (setter booking)
Benchmark ideal 2024: 65-80% show rate
Exemplo de low show rate viável: 35% com offer de $50,000
Frequência de email recomendada: 10-15 emails em 48-72 horas
Frequência de conteúdo recomendada: 8+ impressões por canal (email + social)
Frequência SMS: 4-6 mensagens max em 72 horas
Intervalo entre emails: 2-4 horas (concentrado durante o dia)
Campanha "Hammer Them": $6,300 spend, $0.89 cost per thru-play
Alcance: 7,100 pessoas, frequência 9, 24 vídeos, 72 horas
Audiência dinâmica: 14 dias rolling (HubSpot)
Offer da Cody: $10K-$24K (ensino de compra de negócios)
Case study trader: $100 → $379,000 em 3 meses
Webinar result: 38,000 registrants, $7M revenue, produto $3,500
Win rate exemplo: 86% profitable trades
Testimonial ideal: $3,000 primeiro payout (não $20K+)
Investimento penthouse: $25K/mês × 4 = $100K move-in
Match rate audiência: tipicamente menor que lista original (precisa 200-300 para ter 100 targetable)
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Mindset Fundamental:
Vender o agendamento ≠ Vender o comparecimento
Duas vendas separadas no processo
Pessoa NÃO está vendida em aparecer só porque agendou
Estratégia "Hammer Them":
Atacar como "hornets nest" de todos os ângulos
Audiência tight de quem já tomou ação específica
24 vídeos de framing em loop
Diferente da estratégia "Tornado" (mais ampla)
Três Tipos de Conteúdo:
Framing: Ensinar formas de pensar que beneficiam o ciclo de compra
Objection Handling: Superar barreiras específicas
Testimonials: Prova social estratégica
Seleção de Testimonials:
NÃO usar os "big dogs" mais avançados
Usar pessoas 2-3 steps à frente do prospect
Testimonials que superam objeções específicas do buyer
Exemplo: controle emocional > grande payout
Exemplo: $3,000 primeiro resultado > $20,000 avançado
Familiarity Bias:
Objetivo: frequência 8+ por canal
Email: 8+ impressões
Facebook/Instagram: 8+ impressões
Estabelecer familiaridade massiva em 48-72h
Propaganda Machine:
Empresa deve ser sua própria máquina de propaganda positiva
Prospects pesquisam durante research phase (7 dias exemplo)
Busca comum: "[Nome] Reddit" para reviews não-tendenciosas
66% das pessoas fazem isso
Vácuo de informação = preenchido por propaganda negativa
Balança: Positivo deve superar massivamente Negativo + Neutro
Gestão de Reputação:
Mobilizar comunidade de estudantes no Reddit
Responder a threads negativos com experiências reais
SEO para empurrar negativos para baixo
Criar volume de conteúdo positivo orgânico
Framing Específico para Trading:
Consistência: mostrar trade journal com 86% win rate
Lucratividade: demonstrar payouts reais
Funded status: provar que está funded
Atacar objeções: emotional trading, revenge trading
"Anomaly objection": mostrar pessoas próximas ao nível do prospect
Sofisticação de Mercado:
Lower sophistication: maior frequência, mais agressivo
Higher sophistication (investment/capital raise): menor frequência, vocabulário diferente, PPMs, decks, compliance SEC
Quanto mais ocupada a pessoa, maior a frequência necessária
Best Practices Vendedores:
Manual outreach pré-call obrigatório
Estabelecer comunicação via texto antes da call
Selfie videos (não automatizado)
iPhone obrigatório (nunca Android ou CRM phone)
Textos que não parecem automatizados
ESTRUTURA:
Sequência de Email (10-15 em 48-72h):
Email 1: Confirmação + dados da call + calendário
Email 2+: Imediatamente entrar em framing
Não parecem "você agendou uma call"
Parecem "estou te ensinando a pensar"
Distribuição: concentrada durante o dia (evitar 4 emails ao acordar)
Cada email com ângulo diferente de ataque
Sequência SMS (4-6 em 72h):
Uma frase + link para vídeo
Lembretes
Mais leve que email (pessoas se irritam mais)
Nunca de CRM ou Android
24 Vídeos de Framing (Exemplo Cody):
Video 1: Regras para novos compradores de negócios
Video 2: Mindset de riqueza (401k, ações, imóveis)
Video 3: American Dream está vivo
Video 4: 3 negócios específicos que compraria
Video 5: Boring businesses (lavanderias, car wash, pressure washing)
Video 6: Resposta a haters (motivacional)
Video 7-24: Variações de framing, objeções, educação
Todos postados organicamente
Todos em remarketing para audiência dinâmica
Captions com CTAs (comment word, manychat automation)
Audiência Dinâmica (HubSpot):
Adicionada: quando agenda call
Removida: 14 dias após agendar
Mínimo 100 pessoas para rodar (precisa 200-300 na lista)
Sincronizada com Facebook Ads
Rolling audience constante
Estrutura de Objeções para Trading:
Emotional trading
Revenge trading
Blown accounts history
Money objection (já gastou muito)
"You're an anomaly" (não consigo fazer)
Skepticism
"You got lucky"
Research Phase Protection:
Assumir 7 dias de research (ou tempo até call)
Preencher com propaganda própria
Monitorar Reddit threads
Responder negatividade proativamente
Criar volume de positivo que supera negativo
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Jesse's offer: "couple thousand bucks" (não especificado exato)
Cody's offer: $10K-$24K
Investment offers: diferentes (mais compliance, menos frequência)
Case study webinar: $3,500 product → $7M revenue em 1 webinar
Prop firms: keep 10% dos earnings, pagam 90% ao trader
Primeiro payout exemplo: $2,000-$3,000 (testimonial ideal)
Penthouse Miami: $25K/mês rent, $100K move-in
Ad spend exemplo: $6,300 para audiência de centenas
Cost per thru-play: $0.89
DURAÇÃO: ~66 minutos FORMATO: Webinar interno / Coaching call com análise de caso real (Jesse's trading program show rate problem) + demonstração de estratégia "Hammer Them" com exemplo de cliente (Cody - business buying)
